summary: Myelinated nerves conduct impulses faster at up to 150 m/s via saltatory conduction; thus, stating they are generally slower would be false under normal conditions without specific qualifiers indicating damage or pathology affecting myelin function. The correct answer highlights a misconception about typical myelination effects on neural signaling efficiency. To summarize concisely while focusing only on critical aspects related to the query posed by "Which one isn't accurate," we emphasize core facts regarding comparative conduction speeds between types
